title: *, cw, then . . . — The Replace Loop
subtitle: Search a word, change one, repeat with dot.
summary: *cwNEW Esc, then n.n.n. — change every occurrence of a word, one at a time, with confirmation. The most-loved Vim refactoring loop.

blocks.text: The classic Vim refactoring loop. Searches for a word, changes one instance, then jumps to each next match where you decide whether to dot-repeat (apply) or skip.

sequence.note: Cursor on word: search next exact match
Change word — Vim drops you in Insert
Back to Normal — change is saved as the dot-target
Skip this one — just move to next

blocks.title: Why It's Better Than :%s
blocks.text:
{key::}{key:%}{key:s}{key:/old/new/gc} also works — but you have to type both `old` and `new`, and you confirm at the *line level* (yes/no). The {key:*}{key:c}{key:w} loop is faster:

  • {key:*} **fills in the search pattern for you** (the word under cursor with `\<...\>` boundaries).
  • {key:c}{key:w} drops you straight into Insert at the word's position.
  • Esc commits the change. The {key:.} register now holds *the entire change*, including motion and inserted text.
  • {key:n} + {key:.} cycle through matches, applying or skipping at each.

The loop also handles cases where the matches differ slightly (different surrounding context, decision per-match) — much more flexible than substitute.
Need word boundaries to be loose? Use {key:g}{key:*} instead of {key:*} — finds the word as a substring, not just whole-word matches.
